executive_brief:
The zero-day timeline has collapsed. For the first time in history, AI-driven vulnerability discovery is outpacing human patching cycles, creating a landscape where critical flaws are weaponized within hours of disclosure. This week, Anthropic's 'Claude Mythos' AI identified thousands of zero-days across major infrastructure, while threat actors like Storm-1175 deployed ransomware within 24 hours of vulnerability disclosure [132, 77]. The supply chain is no longer a secondary concern; it is the primary vector. High-profile compromises of Axios, Smart Slider 3 Pro, and CPUID demonstrate that trust in package registries and update mechanisms is broken [1, 257, 302]. 

Simultaneously, the physical world is under direct digital assault. Iran-linked actors have moved beyond espionage to actively disrupting US critical infrastructure by compromising internet-exposed Programmable Logic Controllers (PLCs) [142, 154]. This is not theoretical risk; water and energy facilities are being manipulated. For security leaders, the old model of 'patch Tuesday' is obsolete. The new reality demands automated, AI-driven defense and a 'zero-trust' architecture that assumes every update, every AI agent, and every third-party component is hostile until proven otherwise.

threat_stories.headline: The AI Arms Race: Mythos Discovers Zero-Days Faster Than Humans Can Patch
threat_stories.narrative:
The cybersecurity landscape has fundamentally shifted this week with the public revelation of Anthropic's 'Claude Mythos' AI model. Under Project Glasswing, this AI autonomously identified thousands of high-severity vulnerabilities across major operating systems and browsers, including a 27-year-old flaw in OpenBSD [132, 145]. This capability signifies the end of the traditional vulnerability lifecycle. If AI can find these flaws, adversaries possess the same tools. We are witnessing the acceleration of the 'zero-day timeline' where the window between discovery and exploitation has compressed to near zero [138].

Concurrently, attackers are weaponizing AI against enterprise environments. A critical vulnerability in Flowise (CVE-2025-59528), an open-source AI agent builder, is being actively exploited for remote code execution [76, 129]. Attackers are also leveraging 'GrafanaGhost,' a chained exploit using indirect prompt injection on AI dashboards to exfiltrate data without authentication [95, 107]. These are not isolated incidents; they represent a new class of 'Agentic Attacks' where the software itself becomes the weapon. The implication is clear: AI governance is now a security imperative, not an IT preference. Organizations deploying AI agents without strict sandboxing and input validation are handing attackers direct access to their data.

timeline.event: Anthropic announces Claude Mythos findings under Project Glasswing
timeline.significance: Confirms AI can discover vulnerabilities at scale, compressing the defense window.

timeline.event: Flowise CVE-2025-59528 disclosed and immediately exploited
timeline.significance: Demonstrates active exploitation of AI platform vulnerabilities in the wild.

timeline.event: GrafanaGhost zero-click data exfiltration technique identified
timeline.significance: Shows AI dashboards can be weaponized for silent data theft.

threat_stories.impact_assessment: Critical. Any organization using AI agents, LLMs, or AI-driven dashboards is exposed to prompt injection and code execution risks. The speed of discovery means patches will always lag behind exploits.
threat_stories.action_required: Disable CustomMCP nodes in Flowise immediately. Implement strict input sanitization for all AI agent web browsing. Audit all AI-generated code for hardcoded credentials. Enforce 'human-in-the-loop' verification for agent actions.

threat_stories.headline: Supply Chain Collapse: Axios, Smart Slider, and the Death of Trust
threat_stories.narrative:
The software supply chain is under sustained, coordinated assault. This week, the widely used Axios NPM package was compromised via a hijacked maintainer account, injecting malicious code into downstream applications [1, 10]. Simultaneously, the Smart Slider 3 Pro plugin for WordPress and Joomla was hijacked, distributing backdoored updates to over 800,000 installations [252, 257]. Even hardware utility vendors like CPUID were breached, with their legitimate download servers serving malware [302]. These are not opportunistic hacks; they are targeted operations designed to poison the ecosystem's foundation.

The pattern is consistent: attackers target the 'trusted' update mechanisms that organizations rely on for security. By compromising the maintainer or the update server, they bypass perimeter defenses entirely. The Axios attack specifically targeted the npm trust infrastructure, while the Smart Slider attack utilized compromised Nextend servers [257]. This indicates a shift towards 'poisoned update' campaigns where the patch itself is the payload. Defenders can no longer assume that a package from a public registry is safe. The integrity of the software delivery pipeline is now the primary attack surface.

timeline.event: Axios NPM maintainer account hijacked, malicious versions pushed
timeline.significance: Compromises a foundational dependency for thousands of applications.

timeline.event: Smart Slider 3 Pro update system hijacked, backdoor distributed
timeline.significance: Poisons the update mechanism for 800,000+ WordPress/Joomla sites.

timeline.event: CPUID website compromised, malware served via HWMonitor downloads
timeline.significance: Demonstrates supply chain attacks targeting hardware utility software.

threat_stories.impact_assessment: Critical. Any organization using npm packages, WordPress plugins, or third-party update mechanisms is at risk. The blast radius includes development environments and production systems alike.
threat_stories.action_required: Pin all npm dependency versions to known good hashes. Disable automatic plugin updates for WordPress/Joomla. Verify file integrity of all third-party downloads using checksums. Implement private registries for internal packages.

threat_stories.headline: Storm-1175: The 24-Hour Ransomware Kill Chain
threat_stories.narrative:
Ransomware operations have evolved into high-velocity strikes. Storm-1175, linked to the Medusa ransomware group, is now deploying encryption within 24 hours of vulnerability disclosure [22, 77]. This group exploits a combination of zero-day and N-day vulnerabilities across enterprise products, chaining exploits to bypass security controls and move laterally with unprecedented speed. Microsoft has confirmed that Storm-1175 leverages over 16 vulnerabilities since 2023, often weaponizing fresh bugs before patches are widely deployed [77].

This 'speed-to-exploit' capability renders traditional patch management cycles ineffective. By the time a vulnerability is patched, Storm-1175 has already moved to the next target. The group targets healthcare, education, and critical infrastructure in the UK, US, and Australia [22]. The operational model suggests a dedicated exploit development team working in parallel with reconnaissance, allowing them to strike the moment a flaw is publicized. For defenders, the 'patch window' has closed; isolation and segmentation are now the only viable controls.

timeline.event: Microsoft links Medusa affiliate Storm-1175 to zero-day attacks
timeline.significance: Confirms state-level capability in criminal ransomware operations.

timeline.event: Storm-1175 deploys Medusa ransomware within 24 hours of flaw disclosure
timeline.significance: Demonstrates the collapse of the remediation timeline.

timeline.event: Microsoft warns of accelerated exploitation timelines
timeline.significance: Validates the industry-wide shift in attack velocity.

threat_stories.impact_assessment: Critical. Organizations relying on standard patch cycles are guaranteed compromise. Healthcare and education sectors are primary targets.
threat_stories.action_required: Implement network segmentation to limit lateral movement. Deploy EDR with behavioral analysis to detect rapid privilege escalation. Maintain offline, immutable backups. Prioritize patching based on exploit availability, not just severity.

threat_stories.headline: Iran Targets US Critical Infrastructure via Internet-Exposed PLCs
threat_stories.narrative:
Iran-affiliated threat actors have escalated from espionage to active disruption of US critical infrastructure. A joint FBI/CISA advisory confirms attacks against internet-exposed Programmable Logic Controllers (PLCs) from Rockwell Automation and Siemens [142, 154]. Attackers gain initial access via misconfigured devices, extract project files, and manipulate SCADA/HMI displays to cause operational disruption. This is not a theoretical threat; water, energy, and utility facilities have been compromised [148, 286].

The vulnerability lies in the design and deployment of OT devices. Many PLCs are left directly exposed to the public internet using legacy protocols like Modbus and DNP3 that lack authentication [204]. Attackers utilize leased overseas infrastructure to connect to these devices, often bypassing perimeter defenses entirely. The goal is physical disruption, not just data theft. This campaign highlights the dangerous intersection of IT and OT security, where a network misconfiguration can lead to real-world physical consequences.

timeline.event: FBI/CISA joint advisory warns of Iran targeting OT devices
timeline.significance: Official government acknowledgment of active OT disruption.

timeline.event: Iran-linked actors disrupt US critical infrastructure via PLC attacks
timeline.significance: Confirms successful disruption of water and energy facilities.

timeline.event: Nearly 4,000 US industrial devices found exposed to Iranian cyberattacks
timeline.significance: Quantifies the scale of the exposure.

threat_stories.impact_assessment: Critical. Energy, water, and manufacturing sectors face immediate risk of physical disruption. Internet-exposed PLCs are the primary vector.
threat_stories.action_required: Disconnect PLCs from the internet immediately. Implement network segmentation to isolate OT environments. Block traffic on OT-associated ports (502, 44818, 2222) from overseas IPs. Enable multifactor authentication on all OT management interfaces.

threat_stories.headline: The Zero-Day Tsunami: Fortinet, Adobe, and Ivanti Under Fire
threat_stories.narrative:
A wave of critical zero-days has struck enterprise security infrastructure this week. Fortinet released an emergency patch for CVE-2026-35616 in FortiClient EMS, which allows unauthenticated remote code execution [8, 121]. Simultaneously, Ivanti EPMM (CVE-2026-1340) was added to the CISA Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog [189]. Adobe Reader remains vulnerable to an unpatched zero-day exploited via malicious PDFs since December [242, 258]. These vulnerabilities are not isolated; they target the very tools organizations use to secure themselves.

The Fortinet flaw is particularly dangerous as it affects the management server controlling endpoint security, effectively handing attackers control over the defense infrastructure. The Adobe zero-day exploits the JavaScript engine to fingerprint systems and exfiltrate data without user interaction [242]. The Ivanti vulnerability allows code injection in mobile management systems. This cluster of critical flaws indicates a coordinated pressure on enterprise security stacks. If an organization is running unpatched versions of these tools, they are effectively running without security.

timeline.significance: Critical management server vulnerability patched after active exploitation.

timeline.event: CISA adds Ivanti EPMM flaw to KEV catalog
timeline.significance: Federal mandate to patch within days.

timeline.event: Adobe Reader zero-day confirmed active since December

threat_stories.impact_assessment: Critical. Security operations centers (SOCs) using Fortinet or Ivanti are at immediate risk of compromise. Adobe users are exposed to silent data theft.
threat_stories.action_required: Patch FortiClient EMS to version 7.4.7 immediately. Apply Ivanti EPMM patches. Disable JavaScript in Adobe Reader. Monitor logs for unauthorized API access to management consoles.

actor_profiles.name: Storm-1175
actor_profiles.recent_activity: Deploying Medusa ransomware within 24 hours of vulnerability disclosure. Exploiting zero-days and N-day flaws across enterprise products.

actor_profiles.name: Lazarus Group
actor_profiles.recent_activity: Stole $285M from Drift Protocol via 6-month social engineering operation. Compromised Axios NPM maintainer account. Targeting Node.js maintainers.

actor_profiles.name: APT28
actor_profiles.recent_activity: Hijacking SOHO routers (MikroTik, TP-Link) for DNS hijacking and AitM attacks. Deploying PRISMEX malware against Ukraine and NATO.

actor_profiles.name: Iran-Nexus Threat Actor
actor_profiles.recent_activity: Targeting internet-exposed PLCs (Rockwell, Siemens) in US critical infrastructure. Password spraying against Israeli Microsoft 365 orgs.

actor_profiles.name: UNC1069
actor_profiles.recent_activity: Targeting Node.js maintainers via fake LinkedIn and Slack profiles. Spreading malicious packages across npm, PyPI, Go, Rust.

critical_vulnerabilities.description: Improper access control allows unauthenticated attackers to bypass API authentication and execute arbitrary code.
critical_vulnerabilities.remediation: Apply emergency hotfix for EMS 7.4.5/7.4.6 or upgrade to 7.4.7.

critical_vulnerabilities.description: Code injection in CustomMCP node allows arbitrary JavaScript execution with full Node.js privileges.
critical_vulnerabilities.remediation: Upgrade to Flowise version 3.0.6 or later.

critical_vulnerabilities.description: Code injection vulnerability allowing unauthenticated remote code execution.
critical_vulnerabilities.remediation: Apply latest patch released by Ivanti immediately.

critical_vulnerabilities.description: Authorization bypass allowing attackers to gain root-level access to host systems via oversized HTTP requests.
critical_vulnerabilities.remediation: Update Docker Engine to version 29.3.1 or later.

critical_vulnerabilities.description: Pre-authenticated remote code execution vulnerability exploited within 10 hours of disclosure.
critical_vulnerabilities.remediation: Upgrade Marimo to the latest patched version immediately.

critical_vulnerabilities.description: RCE via Jolokia API management plane, exploitable via default credentials or chained with CVE-2024-32114.
critical_vulnerabilities.remediation: Upgrade to ActiveMQ Classic version 5.19.4 or 6.2.3.

critical_vulnerabilities.description: RTF file exploitation bypassing security controls to force WebDAV connection for payload delivery.
critical_vulnerabilities.remediation: Apply security patches released after January 26, 2026.

critical_vulnerabilities.description: JavaScript engine vulnerability allowing system fingerprinting and data exfiltration via malicious PDFs.
critical_vulnerabilities.remediation: Disable JavaScript in Adobe Reader settings. Avoid opening unsolicited PDFs.

hunting_leads.title: Fortinet EMS API Abuse
hunting_leads.context: Attackers are exploiting CVE-2026-35616 to bypass authentication on FortiClient EMS management servers.
hunting_leads.query: CommonLog | where EventCode == 'API_AUTH_FAILURE' and DestinationIP == 'EMS_SERVER_IP' | summarize count() by SourceIP
indicators: Failed login attempts from overseas IPs

hunting_leads.title: Flowise CustomMCP Node Execution
hunting_leads.context: CVE-2025-59528 allows arbitrary code execution via the CustomMCP node in Flowise.
hunting_leads.query: ProcessCreation | where ParentImage =~ 'node' and CommandLine contains 'CustomMCP' | where CommandLine contains 'child_process'
indicators: Unusual outbound connections from Flowise instances

hunting_leads.title: PLC Traffic to Overseas IPs
hunting_leads.context: Iran-linked actors are connecting to internet-exposed PLCs via leased overseas infrastructure.
hunting_leads.query: NetworkFlow | where DestinationPort in (502, 44818, 2222) and SourceCountry != 'US' | summarize count() by DestinationIP
indicators: Connections to Rockwell/Siemens devices from overseas

hunting_leads.title: LNK File Execution via GitHub
hunting_leads.context: DPRK actors are using GitHub as C2 and LNK files for payload delivery.
hunting_leads.query: ProcessCreation | where FileName == '.lnk' and CommandLine contains 'github.com' | where CommandLine contains 'powershell'
indicators: Outbound traffic to GitHub API from non-dev systems

hunting_leads.title: Marimo RCE Indicators
hunting_leads.context: CVE-2026-39987 is being exploited within hours of disclosure.
hunting_leads.query: ProcessCreation | where Image =~ 'marimo' and CommandLine contains 'eval' | where CommandLine contains 'exec'
indicators: Unexpected file creation in Marimo directories

statistics.key_changes: The time-to-exploit for critical vulnerabilities has collapsed from 61 days to 28.5 days. AI-driven vulnerability discovery is now outpacing human patching cycles.
emerging_trends: AI agents being weaponized for data exfiltration (GrafanaGhost)
Supply chain poisoning of update mechanisms (Smart Slider, Axios)
OT devices targeted for physical disruption (Iran PLC attacks)
Ransomware deployment within 24 hours of disclosure (Storm-1175)
declining_threats: Traditional phishing emails (replaced by QR codes and device code phishing)
Simple credential stuffing (replaced by MFA fatigue and downgrade attacks)
